segments.s264-g1: وزآن is a fused classical prepositional phrase (و + زِ آن) meaning 'and/from that (place)'; جایگه is the noun 'place' that it governs. روی برگاشتند is literal 'they turned their face(s)' — a common Persian idiom meaning 'they turned/returned'; برگاشتند is past tense, 3rd person plural.
segments.s264-g2: به شب دشت پیکار strings several locative modifiers: به شب = 'at night/into the night', دشت = 'plain', پیکار = 'battle'. بگذاشتند is past, 3rd person plural; here it means 'they left/abandoned' (i.e. ceased the fighting or departed from the battlefield). Word order is poetic but modifiers follow the noun they modify.
segments.s264-g3: به + لشکرگه = 'to the camp'; خویش is the possessive 'their/own' split here into خوی + ش (an older orthography). بازآمدند is past tense, 3rd person plural 'they returned/returned back.' The whole phrase = 'they came back to their camp.'
segments.s264-g4: بر پهلوانان = 'to/upon the champions' (بر + accusative target); فراز آمدند is a poetic verbal phrase meaning 'they came up/advanced' or 'approached' (آمدند is past plural). The order places the goal (پهلوانان) before the motion verb, typical in older style.
segments.s264-g5: همه شب = 'the whole night' (همه here 'entire/whole'); به خواب اندر = literary order for 'in sleep/while asleep' (به ... اندر = 'into/within'). آسیب و شیب are poetic nouns indicating trouble or distress; the brief clause places the temporal/locative 'all night in sleep' before the following result clause.
segments.s264-g6: ز پیکارشان is causal — 'because of/from their fighting.' دل‌شده is a compound participial/adjectival form meaning 'heart-made' → 'distraught' (past participle used adjectivally). ناشکیب is an adjective with negative prefix (نا-) meaning 'impatient/restless.' The clause states a resultant emotional state produced by the battle.
segments.s264-g7: سپیده چو = 'when the dawn...' (چو = 'when/as'); از کوه سر بردمید is a poetically phrased action: از کوه = 'from the mountain', سر = 'head/top', بردمید is an older/poetic past form meaning 'lifted/raised' (here applied to dawn). The clause is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو.
segments.s264-g8: شد is past 'became' or auxiliary 'it came to pass'; آن دامن تیره شب = 'that dark hem/skirt of night' (دامن used metaphorically for the edge/veil of night). ناپدید is a past/adjectival predicate 'vanished/was gone.' The whole clause states the result: the darkness disappeared.
segments.s264-g129: جگر خسته هومان بیامد چو زاغ = literal word order is noun (جگر) + adjective (خسته) + proper name (هومان) + past verb (بیامد) + simile marker (چو) + simile noun (زاغ). جگر here is used idiomatically for 'heart/seat of feeling' and بیامد (past of آمدن) is used idiomatically — 'his heart came' = 'his heart sank / he was overwhelmed'. چو introduces the simile 'like a crow.'
segments.s264-g130: سیه گشت از درد رخ چون چراغ = verb گشت (become/turn) follows the adjective سیه; از + درد indicates cause ('from/because of pain'); رخ is the affected noun 'face/visage'; چون introduces another simile ('like a...') with چراغ. The clause thus says the face turned dark from pain, and is compared (simile) to a lamp—the two images are juxtaposed poetically.
segments.s264-g131: بدان خستگی باز جنگ آمدند = بدان (poetic contraction of به + آن / 'because of that' or 'to that') + خستگی (fatigue) marks the reason; باز (again) modifies آمدند (they came/resumed) and جنگ is the subject of آمدند ('the fighting resumed' / 'they came to battle'). The clause states cause ('because of that fatigue') then reports that battle resumed.
segments.s264-g132: گرازان به سان پلنگ آمدند = گرازان (boars) is the subject; به سان (fixed prepositional phrase 'in the manner of / like') governs the simile noun پلنگ (leopard); آمدند is the plural past verb 'came/charged.' The construction describes the boars attacking in a manner likened to leopards.
segments.s264-g133: همی زور کرد این بر آن آن بر این = همی is a durative/continuous particle ('kept, continuously'); زور (strength) is the object of کرد (did/exerted); the two phrases این بر آن and آن بر این ('this against that' and 'that against this') are parallel prepositional phrases with بر meaning 'against.' The overall pattern expresses an ongoing struggle between the two.
segments.s264-g134: گه این را بسودی گه آن را زمین = گه ('sometimes') introduces alternating results; این/آن are the demonstratives ('this'/'that'), and را is the Persian definite/object marker marking the affected party. بسودی is a verb/adverbial form meaning 'it/that availed / prevailed,' while زمین (literally 'ground') here is used with کردن/افتادن implied — '(one) brought the other to the ground.' The clause contrasts occasional outcomes.
segments.s264-g135: ز بیژن فزون بود هومان به زور = ز (poetic 'from/than') introduces the comparison 'than Bijan'; فزون بود (was greater/more abundant) is the comparative predicate; هومان is the subject and به زور ('in/with strength') specifies the dimension of comparison. The clause therefore states that Hōmān exceeded Bijan in strength.
segments.s264-g136: هنر عیب گردد چو برگشت هور = هنر (skill/virtue) is the subject; گرد (here as گردد/گشت 'become') is the present/future-like copular verb 'becomes'; عیب is the predicative complement 'a fault.' چو ('when') introduces the subordinate time clause برگشت هور — 'when Hoor/brightness returns.' The structure says that prowess turns into a defect upon the return of 'Hoor' (read as 'radiance' or a named figure depending on context).
segments.s264-g217: سوی پهلوان (prepositional phrase) + روی برگاشتند (verb phrase). 'سوی' is a preposition placed before its object and here means 'toward(s)'; 'پهلوان' is the goal 'the hero'. 'روی' ('face') appears before the verb as the direct object (their faces), and 'برگاشتند' is past tense, 3rd person plural, meaning 'they turned (their faces)' — the possessive is implicit. Word order (Goal + object + verb) is a normal poetic inversion.
segments.s264-g218: This is a single clause 'وز آن دیده گه نعره برداشتند' where 'وز' is a poetic/contracted preposition equivalent to 'از' ('from/whence'), 'آن' = 'that', and 'دیده گه' = 'watch-place/lookout' (compound). 'نعره برداشتند' is an idiom: 'they took up a shout' → 'they raised a shout'; 'برداشتند' is past 3rd plural.
segments.s264-g219: 'وز آنجا' = 'from there' (poetic 'از'). 'هیونی' is a noun meaning a beast/monster; it is the subject of the clause. 'به سان نوند' is a simile: 'به سان' = 'like/as' and 'نوند' is used here as a proper name/term (likely the musical mode Navand or a traditional simile), so the beast is being compared to 'nāvand' in manner or sound.
segments.s264-g220: 'طلایه سوی پهلوان برفگند' has 'طلایه' (vanguard/forefront) as subject, 'سوی پهلوان' ('toward the hero') as its directional complement, and 'برفگند' is a past action indicating they rushed/charged toward him. The verb is in the past; the object 'پهلوان' follows the preposition 'سوی'. (Note: 'برفگند' here functions as the verb 'they hurled/rushed' in context.)
segments.s264-g221: 'که' introduces the clause describing Bijan. 'بیژن' is the subject; 'به پیروزی آمد' is a fixed expression 'came into victory' = 'came triumphant' (past 3rd singular). 'چو شیر' is a simile 'like a lion' modifying his arrival; 'چو' here means 'as/like.'
segments.s264-g222: 'درفش سیه را' is the definite object with the accusative marker 'را' ('the black banner'); 'سر آورده' is a perfect participle phrase literally 'having brought (its) head' — here idiomatically 'having lowered/put the head (of the banner) beneath'. 'زیر' ('under/beneath') completes the phrase; the whole is a participial clause describing Bijan's action (he caused the black banner's head to be lowered).
segments.s264-g223: 'چو' = 'when/as'. 'دیوانگان گیو' is a genitive construction read in poetry without the ezāfe: 'the madmen of Giv' (i.e., Giv's crazed/ferocious men). 'گشته' is the perfect participle/auxiliary meaning 'have become' or 'became', and 'نوان' is an adjective 'youthful/energetic' — together 'گشته نوان' = 'became vigorous/young'.
segments.s264-g224: This is a paired, emphatic adverbial: 'به هر سو خروشان' = 'to every direction, roaring' and 'هر سو دوان' = 'to every direction, running/rushing'. 'به هر سو' is a directional prepositional phrase repeated for emphasis; 'خروشان' and 'دوان' are participial/adjectival forms describing how they moved.
segments.s264-g17: فرستاده (subject) + برگشت (past verb) is a simple past clause 'the messenger returned'. The conjunction و links to a following clause: با او بگفت, where با او is a prepositional phrase 'with him' indicating the interlocutor and بگفت is the older/narrative past form of گفت ('said, spoke') that typically introduces reported speech (hence the following که-clause).
segments.s264-g18: که is the complementizer introducing the reported clause. با جان پاکت is a prepositional phrase 'with/by your pure soul' (جان = 'soul/life', پاکت = 'your pure' with the enclitic -ت). خرد باد جفت is an optative formula: خرد ('wisdom') is the subject, باد is the archaic optative particle 'may it be', and جفت ('mate/pair') is a predicate meaning 'be a match/paired (with)'.
segments.s264-g19: سپهدار هومان is the nominal subject 'Commander Homan'. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. چو گرد is a postverbal circumstantial phrase: چو 'as/like/when' used here to mark manner and گرد 'around/about' (i.e. 'came circling' or 'came around').
segments.s264-g20: بدان functions as a locative/deictic 'to/there' (formed from the prefix ب- + a deictic); تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking source or opponent (here 'from Bijan' = 'against/with Bijan'), بجوید is the present/subjunctive form 'may seek / seeks' used in the purpose clause, and نبرد is the object 'battle'. Overall: '...came there in order to seek battle (with) Bijan.'
segments.s264-g21: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is the past 'heard' (3rd person), with بیژن as the subject. بیامد is the following past verb 'came'. دمان is a locative noun 'daman' (= 'skirt/foot/foothill'), so the clause reads 'When Bijan heard, he came to the foothill.'
segments.s264-g22: بسیچیده is a past participle/adjective 'well‑arranged, mustered' modifying جنگ ('battle/army'), so بسیچیده جنگ = 'his forces well mustered' or 'battle arrayed'. با ترجمان is an accompanying/instrumental phrase 'with an interpreter/translator' (با = 'with', ترجمان = 'interpreter'), indicating accompaniment or that an interpreter/liaison was present.
segments.s264-g23: به پشت is a locative phrase 'on/at the back'. شباهنگ here names an item placed at/onto the back (literally 'night‑song'/'nightingale' used poetically for a dark/ornamental cloak or piece). بر بسته is a compound of the preposition بر ('on') + بسته (past participle 'bound/tied'), and تنگ ('tight') modifies بسته: 'having bound tightly on his back (the night‑cloak)'.
segments.s264-g24: چو introduces a simile ('as/like'). جنگی is adjectival 'warlike' or 'battle‑ready', پلنگی 'leopard‑like/spot‑patterned' characterizes the fighter image, گرازان is a plural noun 'boars' (or 'a troop of boars'), and به جنگ is the directional phrase 'into battle'. The whole simile says he went into battle like a war‑leopard among the boars.
segments.s264-g89: به کردار آتش is a simile phrase: به + noun (کردار) = 'in the manner/way of'. Here کردار functions as 'manner' and is modified by آتش ('fire'), so the whole phrase means 'in the manner of fire' or 'fiery'. It precedes the noun it modifies (پرندآوران), a typical classical Persian order where the modifying phrase comes before the head noun.
segments.s264-g90: فرو ریخت is a past tense verb 'poured/fell down' with the prefixed adverbial فرو giving the downward sense (often written together as فروریخت). از دست کنداوران is a prepositional agent phrase 'from the hands of ...' where از governs دست and the agent (کنداوران) follows; the verb comes before this prepositional phrase, following SOV tendencies of older Persian.
segments.s264-g91: نبد is an archaic/copular negative meaning 'there was not' or 'did not have'. دسترسشان is the noun 'their reach/access' with the possessive -شان attached. به خون ریختن is an infinitival/verbal-noun phrase ('to shed blood' or 'shedding of blood') with به introducing the verbal noun as the object of دسترس; together: 'they had no access to shedding blood.'
segments.s264-g92: نشد here means 'did not become / did not happen' and combines with the predicate سیر دلشان ('their hearts' + سیر = 'sated/stuffed') so that سیر دلشان نشد = 'their hearts were not sated'. ز + آویختن ('from hanging') uses the verbal noun آویختن to indicate the cause/means (i.e. 'they were not satisfied by hanging'). Note the verb نشد follows the nominal predicate, a common classical order.
segments.s264-g93: عمود is a noun ('pillar' literally, here likely a pike/standard) placed at the start as the object/topic. از پس = 'after' or 'behind', تیغ is 'sword', and برداشتند is the past plural 'they took up/removed'. The clause follows an older Persian word order with the object/topic before the verbal predicate at the end: 'They took up the pike after the sword.'
segments.s264-g94: از اندازه is a phrase meaning 'beyond measure' or 'because of exceeding measure/degree'; پیکار is 'battle' and بگذاشتند is an archaic/semi-analytical past 'they left/abandoned' (بـ + گذاشتند). Together the clause means they ceased/abandoned the fight when it exceeded bounds: 'they left the battle because it went beyond measure.'
segments.s264-g95: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial 'from that time on'. بر آن (upon that) + بر نهادند (they set upon / they placed upon) forms a compound where نهادند (past of نهادن) is the main verb 'they set/established', and the repeated بر gives the sense 'to set about/upon that (task)'. کار is the object 'work/task'. The whole means 'From then on they set to that work.'
segments.s264-g96: که introduces a subordinate purpose/explanation ('so that' / 'namely that'), زور آزمایند is a verbal phrase 'they test/try strength' — آزمایند is present-subjunctive/optative here ('may test' or 'try out'), and در کارزار is an adverbial 'in battle/on the battlefield'. Together: 'that they test their strength in battle.'
segments.s264-g161: روانش (his soul) is the topic; the following روان is an adjectival/nominal comment (poetic repetition) meaning 'at ease' or 'peaceful'. ورا (to it / unto it) introduces the relation that follows, and بنده باد is an optative construction: بنده 'slave/servant' plus the archaic wish-particle باد 'may it be'. The whole segment is a wish ('May his soul be peaceful and may it be a servant to that [peace/spirit]') using poetic inversion and the optative باد.
segments.s264-g162: به چنگال شیران is a prepositional phrase indicating direction/agent ('into/into the claws of the lions'); تنش is the definite object 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش). کنده باد is again a participle/adjective کنده 'torn/detached' with the optative باد 'may it be', so the verb phrase expresses a wish ('may his body be torn'). Word order places the prepositional phrase before the object and uses the archaic optative construction.
segments.s264-g163: سرش را is the definite object ('his head' with accusative marker را). به + فتراک شبرنگ functions as an instrumental/prepositional phrase ('with a variegated saddle-cloth'); بست is the simple past tense verb 'bound'. The sequence is canonical object–prepositional phrase–verb, typical of poetic diction where the instrument follows به.
segments.s264-g164: تنش را is again the definite object 'his body'. به خاک اندر is an older-style prepositional phrase meaning 'into the earth' (به indicating direction and اندر adding 'in/into'), and افگند is the past tense 'threw/cast'. پست is an adverb/adjectival comment 'low/beneath' qualifying how it was cast. The past verb comes after its prepositional complements in this poetic word order.
segments.s264-g165: گشاده سلیح is a compressed adjectival phrase 'weapons wide/spread' (poetic order: adjective before noun), referring to unsheathed or splayed arms. و links the two clauses. گسسته کمر is a participle + noun 'the belt/waist broken' (گسسته 'broken' modifying کمر). These are verbless nominal phrases describing the state of the corpse.
segments.s264-g166: تنش جای دیگر literally 'his body (is) in another place' — Persian here omits the copula and uses جای 'place' plus modifiers. دگر جای سر repeats the pattern for the head: 'another place (is) the head' / 'the head in a different place.' The doubled words (دیگر دگر) are poetic reinforcement meaning 'in a different/other place.'
segments.s264-g167: زمانه 'the age/world' is the subject; سراسر 'throughout/entire' modifies the following noun phrase, so سراسر فریب means 'utterly/entirely deceit.' است is the copula 'is'. و بس ('and that's all' / 'and nothing more') is an emphatic tag that closes the assertion. The phrase states a general judgment: 'The world is all deception.'
segments.s264-g168: به سختی is an adverbial phrase 'with difficulty / scarcely.' نباشدت is an archaic negative past/impersonal form meaning 'there was not' or 'was scarcely' (here conveying 'there was hardly'); فریادرس 'rescuer/succour' is the noun governed by that negation. Together the segment means 'in hardship there was hardly any rescuer,' using older morphology (نباشدت) common in epic poetry.
segments.s264-g169: This clause sets up a conditional/contrast: 'جهان را نمایش' uses the object-marking را with a predicate noun (a poetic structure meaning 'the world is a display/spectacle'). چو functions here as a temporal/conditional conjunction 'when/if'. نیست is the negative copula in the present/past-poetic sense 'is not'. Word order is topic ('جهان را نمایش') + condition ('چو کردار نیست').
segments.s264-g170: سپردن is the infinitive/masdar 'to entrust'; بدو is a clitic/pronominal dative 'to it/him' (referring back to 'the world'); دل is the direct object 'heart' (one's feelings); سزاوار نیست is a two-word predicate meaning 'is not fitting/appropriate'. The whole segment states the evaluative conclusion tied to the previous condition.
segments.s264-g171: بترسید is a past-tense verb form 'feared/was afraid' (3rd-person past in this poetic register). از او marks the object/source of fear 'of him'. یار هومان is a noun phrase 'Hooman's companion' (یار governed by the name). چو دید ('when he saw') connects this reaction to what was seen.
segments.s264-g172: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining what was seen or why the fear occurred ('that...'). بر here functions as 'upon/against' and مهتر او is 'his chief/leader' (مهتر + possessive او). چنان بد رسید literally 'such bad arrived' — رسید is past 'arrived/befell', so the idiom means 'such misfortune befell his chief.'
segments.s264-g173: چو شد is the temporal clause 'when it became / when it happened that'. کار هومان ویسه places the noun 'کار' ('case/affair') before two proper names in apposition ('Hooman and Viseh') to mean 'the affair of Hooman and Viseh'. تباه is the predicate 'ruined/ruinous' so the clause means 'when Hooman and Viseh's affair was ruined.'
segments.s264-g174: دوان is a past verb 'ran/rushed' (here narrating action). ترجمانان is the plural 'interpreters/mediators' as subject. هر دو سپاه literally 'each/both armies' — here functioning together as a genitive/qualification meaning 'of both armies'. The clause therefore reports that the interpreters belonging to both armies ran away.
segments.s264-g175: ستایش‌کنان is an active participle used nominally 'those who praise' or 'the praisers'. پیش بیژن ('before Bijan') places the preposition/prepositional adverb پیش after the noun as is common in poetry. شدند is the past plural 'they came/they became' used here to mean 'they advanced/appeared.'
segments.s264-g176: چو is the comparative 'as/like'. پیش بت چین is 'before the Chinese idol' (بت = idol, چین = China/Chinese used attributively). برهمن شدند 'they became Brahmins' — برهمن (Brahmin) as a simile for ritualistic or hollow worship; شدند is past plural 'they acted like / became'. Together the clause compares the praisers' behavior to Brahmins worshipping an idol.
segments.s264-g49: برانگیختند is a 3rd-person plural past verb (they roused/spurred); its object اسب (horse) follows it in the usual SOV-influenced Persian order here. و simply joins the two coordinated actions. برخاست is another past 3rd-plural verb (‘they rose’), and گرد here is a concrete noun meaning the dust/cloud that rises when horses are driven — so the clause reads literally ‘they spurred the horses and the dust rose.’
segments.s264-g50: This clause places the instrumental/relational phrase first: به زه بر نهاده is a participial construction meaning ‘having set (the bow) on the string’ (به = to/onto, زه = bow‑string, بر نهاده = placed/put on). کمان is the head noun ‘bow’ and نبرد functions as a noun ‘for battle’ (the entire clause: ‘the bow strung on the string for battle’). The verb is omitted; this is a compact, poetic nominal phrase explaining what they prepared.
segments.s264-g51: This noun phrase modifies the warriors’ appearance: دو خونی is an adjectival compound (‘two‑blood’/blood‑red or bloodied — here ‘blood‑red’); برافراخته is a perfect/participle meaning ‘lifted/raised’ and follows the adjective, and سر is the noun ‘head.’ به ماه is a prepositional phrase ‘toward/to the moon’ or a simile (‘as to the moon’), so the whole segment describes heads raised toward the moon (a poetic image). Word order is adjectival phrase + participle + noun + prepositional modifier.
segments.s264-g52: چنان is an adverb ‘so/thus’ and introduces the result clause. کینه‌ور گشته is a compound of the adjective کینه‌ور (‘vengeful/vindictive’) with گشته, the perfect/auxiliary participle ‘has become’ (gَشته = become), yielding ‘has become vindictive.’ از + کین شاه gives the cause: ‘because of the king’s hatred/vengeance.’ Word order is cause after the verb phrase (typical in Persian: result/condition then از + cause).
segments.s264-g53: ز is the poetic short form of از (‘from’) and introduces the origin phrase ز کوه ‘from the mountain.’ کنابد is an older/poetic present/past participial-ish verb here (literary sense ‘strains/works/labors’ or ‘is in turmoil’), but in the clause it is bound to the following main verb تاختند (‘they galloped/rushed’), so the sense is that they burst forth from the mountain; برون is an adverb ‘out/forth’ and تاختند is the 3rd‑pl past ‘they rushed/galloped.’
segments.s264-g54: سران is the plural noun ‘leaders/heads’ (subject of the following verb). سوی is the directional preposition ‘toward,’ هامون is the plain/steppe ‘plain,’ and برافراختند is the 3rd‑person plural past ‘they raised/erected’ (here ‘raised their heads’). The clause is a straightforward SOV order with a prepositional phrase indicating direction.
segments.s264-g55: برفتند is the 3rd‑person plural past ‘they went/departed/rode on.’ چندانک is a subordinating adverb meaning ‘to such an extent/so that,’ and اندر زمی (اندر = in/within, زمی = earthly/land) together mark the extent/location ‘into the land/ground.’ The phrase sets up the extent to which they traveled before the result clause that follows.
segments.s264-g56: ندیدند is the 3rd‑person plural past negative/affirmative ‘they saw’ (here used neutrally in past: ‘they saw not’ in sense). جایی is an indefinite adverb/noun ‘anywhere/some place,’ پی is a preposition meaning ‘after/in search of’ (literally ‘on the trail of’), and آدمی is ‘a man/person.’ The clause therefore means ‘they saw no place in pursuit/of any man’ — idiomatically, ‘they saw no sign of anyone.’
segments.s264-g225: چو/همی here is the narrative particle indicating continuing or repeated action; آگهی آمد is 'news/information' and جست is the past (narrative) verb 'sought/searched for'. ز + آن + نیوپور is a poetic genitive/ablative: 'from that Nivpour'. Word order is Subject (implied) + object (آگهی) + verb (جست) + source phrase (ز آن نیوپور).
segments.s264-g226: همی is again the continuous/emphatic narrative particle. ماتم is a noun 'mourning'; آورد is the past verb 'brought/caused' (here 'caused grief'). هنگام سور is a time phrase 'at the time of the feast/banquet'. The clause means that as a consequence he caused mourning at the banquet.
segments.s264-g227: چو ... آمد is a temporal clause 'when ... came'. آگاهی آمد = 'news/information arrived'; ز بیژن = 'from Bijan'. بدوی here is not straightforward: in context it functions as a verb/adverb meaning 'he hastened/ran' (classical/poetic usage), although the supplied lemma senses do not provide that meaning (see senses field).
segments.s264-g228: This clause describes the physical gesture: دمان (here 'دمان' = the hem/skirt of a garment) + پیش 'before/in front of' + فرزند 'the child/son' gives 'before the son’s hem'; بنهاد is past 'placed/put' and روی is 'face'. Together: 'he placed/pressed his face upon the child's hem.' Word order is object (دامان پیش فرزند) then verb (بنهاد) then the thing placed (روی).
segments.s264-g229: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. چشمش = 'his eye/gaze' (possessive suffix -ش). به + روی گرامی رسید = 'fell upon / reached the honored face' (رسید used intransitively 'reached' / 'came to rest on'). Word order: temporal conjunction + subject (چشمش) + prepositional target (به روی گرامی) + intransitive verb (رسید).
segments.s264-g230: ز اسب اندر آمد is a common classical phrase meaning 'alighted from the horse' (ز = from, اسب = horse, اندر آمد = came down/entered from). چنان چون سزید is a stock idiom 'just as was fitting' or 'as befitted' (سزید in the sense 'befit/deserve'). The second clause therefore says he dismounted in the manner appropriate.
segments.s264-g231: بغلتید (plural past) 'embraced / fell into each other's arms' and is followed by و 'and'. بنهاد is past 'put/placed' and بر خاک سر means 'their head on the earth' (a formulaic act of prostration). Word order: verb (بغلتید) + conjunction + verb (بنهاد) + prepositional object (بر خاک سر).
segments.s264-g232: همی is the habitual/narrative particle. آفرین is an exclamation 'bravo/praise' and خواند here is a past verb 'uttered/called out' (i.e. 'he cried out praise'). بر دادگر = 'for/upon the just one' (دادگر = just ruler/person). The clause means he repeatedly exclaimed praise for the just one.
segments.s264-g233: گرفتش is past tense گرفت + enclitic ـش (him); it means “he took/seized him.” به بر باز is a small locative phrase: به (toward/into) + بر (upon) + باز (back/again), so together they indicate returning someone into (onto) the bosom/arms. فرزند را is the definite object (را marking the noun “the son”) placed after the locative phrase for emphasis.
segments.s264-g234: This is a string of adjectival epithets modifying فرزند and closed by the object marker را. The adjectives are coordinated with و (and): دلیر (brave) و جوان (young) و خردمند (wise). The final را repeats the object marking already on فرزند for added definiteness/emphasis.
segments.s264-g235: وز آنجا here functions as a fused connector (و + ز = “and/from there”) introducing motion; آنجا = “there.” دمان (دامن) literally “hem/skirt” is used metonymically to picture people moving (their hems) toward someone: سوی = “toward,” سالار شاه = “the king’s commander/chief.” The phrase therefore means “from there toward the commander of the king.”
segments.s264-g236: ستایش‌کنان is an adverbial participial form meaning “praising” (they praised while doing the action). برگرفتند is past-plural (they took up / they set out), and راه is the direct object “the road/path” (they ‘took up the road’ = they set out). Word order places the adverbial participle before the verb.
segments.s264-g237: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning “when.” دیدند is past plural “they saw.” مر is an archaic/clitic element (here functioning as a demonstrative/object-clitic) that does not match the modern senses listed; پهلوان را is the definite object “the champion” (را marking it). ز دور = “from afar” (ز = from, دور = distant). The clause reads: “When they saw the champion from afar…”
segments.s264-g238: نبیره is a noun meaning “offspring/scion” and is the subject of the verb phrase فرود آمد, a two-part verbal expression meaning “alighted/dismounted.” از اسب تور is a prepositional phrase “from Tur’s horse” (از = from, اسب = horse, تور = proper name Tūr). Together: “the scion dismounted from Tur’s horse.”
segments.s264-g239: پر از X is an idiomatic construction meaning “full of X.” Here two parallel phrases occur: پر از خون سلیح = “full of the blood of weapons” (i.e., bloodied by arms) and پر از خاک سر = “full of the dust of the head” (i.e., his head covered with dust). The coordination with و links the two balanced “full of …” phrases.
segments.s264-g240: سر گرد هومان is a nominal phrase identifying Homan’s head (سر = head; گرد here = around/about/positioned — a compact poetic word-order). به فتراک بر places that head in relation to the saddle-cloth: به = toward/on, فتراک = saddle-cloth, بر = on/upon. The clause means that Homan’s head lay upon or was turned onto the saddle-cloth (a compact poetic image).
segments.s264-g273: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; رفتی is the 2nd‑person singular past form ('you went/you come') used in classical Persian to mean 'when you come/when you approach'. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the vicinity of' and precedes the head noun لشکر ('army'); فراز is a poetic modifier attached to لشکر ('on high/forward'), so the whole phrase means 'when you approach the army (on its forward/high part)'.
segments.s264-g274: سپه را: سپه 'the host/army' with را marking it as a definite/direct object. یکی is a numeral 'one' meaning 'one unit/detachment'. سوی هومان is a prepositional phrase 'toward Hūmān' (سوی = toward, هومان = proper name). بساز is the 2nd‑person imperative/command form of ساختن ('make/arrange/deploy'), so the speaker commands the addressee to deploy one part of the army toward Hūmān.
segments.s264-g275: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' (showing the indirect object of گفت); گفت is the past tense 'said' introducing reported speech. نستیهن is a proper name (the person to be brought). ایدون is a verbal stem meaning 'come/appear', and کردن (کنم here) is attached as a first‑person singular present/subjunctive that, in classical narrative, expresses the speaker's volition/future action — together ایدون کنم = 'I will cause him to come / I will bring him.'
segments.s264-g276: که introduces the content/purpose clause ('that/so that'). از خون زمین is a prepositional phrase 'from the blood of the earth' (خون modifies زمین in a possessive/partitive sense). رود جیحون is a noun phrase 'the river Jihūn' (رود = 'river' before the proper name), and کنم is again 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کردن meaning 'I will cause/make'. The clause therefore states the intended result: 'that I will make the Jihūn river flow from the earth's blood.'
segments.s264-g121: This is a nominal phrase describing the body’s condition: تن (subject) + از درد (prepositional phrase giving cause, “from/because of pain”) + لرزان (predicate adjective/participle “trembling”). چو introduces a simile (“like/as”), followed by a short clause چو از باد بید = “like a (willow) shaken by the wind.” Word order is typical Persian: noun → cause phrase → adjectival participle; the simile clause follows with چو + prepositional phrase.
segments.s264-g122: Structure: دل (subject) + از جان شیرین (prepositional phrase giving cause/ground, “because of the sweet life/beloved”) + شده (perfect/passive participle ‘has become’) + ناامید (predicate adjective ‘hopeless’). Note that جان شیرین is noun + adjective (typical Persian order), and شده here functions as the auxiliary ‘become/has become.’
segments.s264-g123: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase “to/unto God”; چنین is an adverb “thus/so”; گفت is the past tense verb “he said.” کای is the vocative particle “O” (addressing someone) and کردگار is the vocative noun “Creator/God.” The order is: indirect object (to God) → manner (thus) → verb (said) → direct address (O Creator).
segments.s264-g124: تو دانی is a present tense second-person clause ‘you know’ (تو = you; دانی = you know). نهان من و آشکار lists two nouns/adjectival states governed by that knowledge: نهان من ‘my hidden (things/secrets)’ and آشکار ‘(that which is) manifest/clear’. The possessive is expressed by placing من after نهان (literally ‘the hidden of me’), and و links the paired opposites.
segments.s264-g125: اگر introduces a conditional clause ‘if.’ داد is a noun here meaning ‘justice’ and is the object of بینی (‘you see’). بینی is second-person present (you see / you perceive). همی is an old/poetic particle adding continuity or emphasis (often rendered as ‘indeed’/‘ever’). جنگ ما ‘our war’ is the phrase linked to the following infinitive/imperative idea; Persian often leaves the full verb implied in poetic lines.
segments.s264-g126: بر این کینه جستن is an infinitival/nominal construction: بر (‘against/over’) + این کینه (‘this grudge’) + جستن (infinitive ‘to seek/take up’). The second بر (preposition) introduces بر آهنگ ما ‘against our intention/plan’ (آهنگ = intention/aim). In context the phrase calls for acting against the enemy’s intent; jastan functions as the verbal noun ‘to pursue/seize’ and the prepositions mark the targets.
segments.s264-g127: ز من مگسل is an imperative/negative construction: ز من ‘from me’ + مـگسل. Here مگسل represents an archaic negative imperative (م- prefix + گسل ‘to separate/tear away’) meaning ‘do not separate (him) from me.’ امروز توش مرا places a time adverb ‘today’ and then توش (‘in it/in him’) + مرا (‘me’ as object), i.e. ‘today, do not separate (him) from me; keep me in him.’ Note that old poetic clitics and archaic negative prefixes appear.
segments.s264-g128: نگه‌دار here is an imperative verb ‘keep/preserve’ (though the listed sense included the noun ‘guardian’, the form functions as the verb ‘keep’). بیدار is the adjective ‘awake/alert’ modifying the following object هوش مرا (‘my awareness/wits’), where هوش is the object and مرا (with را understood) marks possession/object. The sequence therefore means ‘keep my awareness awake’ or more idiomatically ‘keep my wits alert.’
segments.s264-g57: نه begins a negative parallel construction ('neither/ nor'). بر آسمان is a locative expression 'over/across the sky' (بر used as 'over'). کرگسان را uses را as the definite/object marker attached to کرگسان ('vultures') and گذر is a noun 'passage/passing' so the phrase means 'the passing of vultures over the sky'.
segments.s264-g58: The second parallel negation introduced by نه applies to this clause. خاکش ('its soil/earth') is the possessed noun, سپرده here is a past participle meaning 'left/was left', and پی شیر نر is a locational/relational phrase 'on the track/footprint (پی) of a male (نر) lion (شیر)'; word order is poetic but the whole means 'its soil was not left in the lion's track'.
segments.s264-g59: نه introduces negation 'no/none'. از لشکران sets the source/group 'from the troops/armies', and یار و فریادرس are coordinated nouns 'companion/ally and rescuer' describing the kinds of people absent; the whole phrase lists who was not present among the forces.
segments.s264-g60: به پیرامن اندر is a fixed locative phrase meaning 'in/around the vicinity' (به + پیرامن + اندر), and ندیدند is the past-tense 3rd-plural 'they did not see'. کس here, combined with the negative verb, yields 'they saw no one' (i.e. there was nobody).
segments.s264-g61: نهادند is the past 3rd-plural of نهادن 'they placed/established' and takes پیمان ('a pact/covenant') as its object. که introduces the subordinate clause; با ترجمان 'with the interpreter' marks the party toward whom the stipulation in the clause applies.
segments.s264-g62: نباشند is a negative subjunctive/optative 'that they should not be' (3rd-plural); در چیرگی 'regarding/in matters of dominance' is a locative/relational phrase, and بدگمان is the predicate adjective 'suspicious/distrustful'. Together: 'that they should not be suspicious about domination.'
segments.s264-g63: بدان تا is an archaic connective meaning roughly 'so that' or 'to this end' (ب- prefix + دان 'to know/let it be known') introducing a purpose/result clause. بد و نیک 'bad and good' are coordinated accusative topics, with با شهریار 'to/with the king' indicating the addressee of the telling.
segments.s264-g64: بگویند is the present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-plural of 'to say/tell' ('that they should tell'). از این گردش روزگار is the prepositional object 'about/of this turning/cycle of time/fate' (گردش روزگار = the turn of fate), specifying the subject matter they are to report to the king.
segments.s264-g249: به + noun + فرمود is an indirect-object fronting: به گنجور = 'to the treasurer/treasury' (the person in charge of the treasure) comes before فرمود ('he ordered/said'). پس here is a linear discourse marker 'then' and پهلوان is vocative or the addressee (the champion) of the ensuing command.
segments.s264-g250: که introduces the reported content or command ('that/that you ...'). تاج آر is best read as a verb phrase where آر = 'to adorn' (classical/poetic infinitive/imperative sense) acting on تاج = 'crown'; با جامه خسروان = 'with the garments of kings' (با = 'with', جامه = 'robe/garment', خسروان = 'kings') complements the verb. The object ('him') is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s264-g251: گهربافته is a compound participial/adjectival form 'jeweled, gem-woven' modifying پیکر ('body'). و links the next noun bōm ('realm/soil'); زر postmodifies بوم giving 'realm of gold'. The adjective phrase precedes the nouns in literary style and applies to the first noun directly and to the second via the coordinating و.
segments.s264-g252: درفشان is an adjective 'shining/glittering' modifying the coordinated nouns تاج و کمر ('crown and girdle'). چو خورشید is a simile 'like the sun' placed before the nouns it qualifies (typical poetic word order). Note کمر here takes the sense 'girdle/belt' rather than 'waist'.
segments.s264-g253: ده + noun is the numeral construction 'ten horses'; آوریدند is the past plural 'they brought'. زرین لگام is a postpositive adjectival phrase 'golden bridles' describing the horses (adjectives or modifiers may follow the verb cluster in classical Persian listing).
segments.s264-g254: This is a parallel nominal phrase: پری‌روی (compound adjective 'fair‑faced') and زرین کمر ('golden‑belted') modify ده غلام ('ten servants/slaves'). Adjectives precede or follow depending on rhythm and emphasis; here both attributes are bundled before the head غلام.
segments.s264-g255: بدو داد و گفت = 'he gave to him and said' with بدو = 'to him' (indirect object clitic-like placement), داد = past 'gave', و گفت = 'and said'. از گه سام شیر is a temporal phrase 'since the time/age of Sam the Lion' (گه = 'time/occasion', سام = proper name, شیر = epithet 'the Lion').
segments.s264-g256: کسی ناورید is a negative past sense: کسی here functions as 'no one' (rather than 'someone'), and ناورید is a past-negative form 'did not bring' / 'has not brought'. اژدهایی = 'a dragon' (indefinite), and به زیر = 'under/beneath' — together meaning 'no one has ever brought a dragon beneath (him)' i.e. 'no one has ever subdued/overthrown a dragon under his rule/hand.'
segments.s264-g193: برفت is a classical past-tense verb meaning 'he went off/rode away' (بر- + رفت); و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بر here is the preposition 'on/upon' governing the following NP. آن دشت is 'that plain' (demonstrative + noun). کرد is the light verb 'did/made' used with a following nominal/exclamatory complement; کرد آفرین together means 'uttered/poured forth praise' or 'shouted "afarin"' — a common poetic collocation where کردن + noun = 'to utter/perform X'.
segments.s264-g194: بر governs the following phrase and means 'upon' or 'concerning'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. بخت بیدار is a noun plus an adjective that follows the noun (بخت = 'fortune/luck', بیدار = 'awake/alert'); in poetry adjectives sometimes shift position but here بیدار qualifies بخت. و links two coordinated items; فرخ زمین means 'fortunate/auspicious earth/land' (فرخ = 'fortunate/auspicious', زمین = 'earth/land'), with a slightly inverted/poetic word order.
segments.s264-g195: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (temporal). آن is 'those/the'. دیده‌بانان لشکر is a NP where دیده‌بانان (sentinels/lookouts) is specified in relation to لشکر (the army) — 'the army's lookouts'. ز دور is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from afar' (ز = 'from', دور = 'distant'). The whole chunk functions as a temporal subordinate clause.
segments.s264-g196: درفش and نشان are two coordinated nouns 'banner/standard and emblem/sign' (objects perceived by the lookouts). سپهدار is a title meaning 'commander' that precedes the proper name تور here as an apposition or title — 'the commander Tur'. The phrase names what the lookouts saw.
segments.s264-g197: بدیدند is the simple past 3pl 'they saw'. ز آن دیده literally 'from that sight/vantage' (ز = 'from', آن = 'that', دیده = 'eye/sight' used here metonymically for 'vantage/lookout'). برخاستند is past 3pl 'they rose up' and follows as the next coordinated verb; Persian often strings past verbs in sequence to describe successive actions.
segments.s264-g198: به شادی is an adverbial phrase 'in/with joy' (به used as 'in' or 'with'). خروشیدن is the infinitival/masdar form 'to cry out/roar' used here as what they were about to do. آراستند is past 3pl of آراستن 'to array/prepare/arrange' and here means 'they prepared/arrayed themselves (for shouting)' — the light verb pattern where an action-infinitive (خروشیدن) is framed by a preparatory verb (آراستند).
segments.s264-g199: طلایه is 'vanguard' (the forward troop) and appears as the topic/subject. هیونی here is an epithet or group-name attached to طلایه (poetic/archaic; contextually 'the Hyuni' or 'the fierce/monstrous [troop]'). برافگند is an older past form meaning 'sent up/raised/let loose' (here 'advanced/issued forth'); زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly' modifying the verb and placed after it (poetic word order).
segments.s264-g200: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'near/approaching'. پیران is the plural 'elders' (here meaning the senior commanders or leaders). به کردار is 'in the manner/like' (به + کردار 'manner, way'), and دود is 'smoke' used metaphorically; the phrase describes their approach as being 'like smoke' — a common poetic simile. Note the repeated به is a stylistic/poetic device.
segments.s264-g201: که here is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' introducing the clause. هومان is the subject (proper name). به + پیروزی شهریار is a destination phrase: به governs the noun پیروزی ('victory') which stands in a genitive relation to شهریار ('the king'), so پیروزی شهریار = 'the king's victory' (understood idiomatically as 'to the victorious king').
segments.s264-g202: دوان آمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'came running / rushed': دوان functions adverbially/participial, followed by the past verb آمد. از مرکز کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from the center of the battle' (از = 'from', مرکز = 'center', کارزار = 'battlefield') and supplies the origin for the motion.
segments.s264-g203: This is a genitive noun phrase: درفش (banner) + سپهدار (of the commander) + ایران (of Iran), followed by نگون as a postpositive adjective meaning 'fallen/ill‑fated.' The sequence reads 'the (ill‑fated / fallen) banner of Iran's commander.'
segments.s264-g204: تنش is 'his body' (تن + enclitic possessive -ش). غرقه مانده is a compound: غرقه 'drenched/immersed' describing the state and مانده 'remained/was left' as a perfect participle indicating position. به خاک اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/onto the inner earth' (به + خاک, with اندرون 'within/inside'), giving where the body lay.
segments.s264-g205: همه لشکرش = 'all his army/host' (همه + لشکر with the possessive suffix -ش). برگرفته خروش is a participial construction: برگرفته 'having taken up/raised' governs خروش 'a cry/roar' — together 'having raised a shout.' The whole segment means 'his whole host, having raised a cry.'
segments.s264-g206: This is an inverted word order where the verb/phrase نهاده (placed/gave) comes before the grammatical subject سپهدار. به هومان is the indirect object 'to Homan,' and گوش (here idiomatically) = 'ear/attention.' Thus سپهدار گوش نهاده = 'the commander lent an ear' and the full segment reads 'the commander gave ear to Homan.'
segments.s264-g207: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' بیژن is the subject. میان دو رویه سپاه is a locative phrase 'between the two ranks/lines of the army' (میان = 'between', دو = 'two', رویه used poetically for 'face/line/rank', سپاه = 'army').
segments.s264-g208: رسید is the past verb 'arrived/reached.' اندر آن سایه is locative 'into/within that shade/shelter.' تاج و گاه are two nouns in apposition joined by و; تاج = 'crown' and گاه poetically 'place/seat' (here 'throne/seat of rule'), so the phrase describes the royal shelter — 'that shade of crown and throne.'
segments.s264-g65: This is a paired interrogative/relative clause introduced by که, here linking two parallel questions: کردار چون بود (literally “how was the conduct”) and پیکار چون (literally “how the battle [was]”). کردار is a nominal subject meaning “conduct/behavior,” چون is the interrogative/adverb “how,” and بود is the past copula “was.” The coordinating و links to the second noun پیکار, which takes the same implied predicate.
segments.s264-g66: چه here is exclamative/interrogative meaning “what/how (a...)”; زاری is the noun “lamentation(s)” and رسید is the past verb “reached/rose” (i.e., came forth). اندر این دشت خون is a locative prepositional phrase (اندر = “in”) — دشت خون = “plain of blood” (blood used attributively). Word order is somewhat poetic: exclamation + noun + verb + location.
segments.s264-g67: بگفتند is a simple past-plural verb “they said” (or “they spoke”), followed by و “and.” ز + اسبان is the poetic agent/ablative “from the horses,” and فرود آمدند is a compound verbal expression meaning “they descended/alighted” (فرود + آمدند = alighted from).
segments.s264-g68: به introduces the target/goal (“to/on”), بند زره is the strap (بند) of the armor (زره) — poetic terse noun phrase — and بر کمر places it on/against the waist (کمر as girdle/belt). برزدند is a past plural verb literally “they struck/struck on,” used idiomatically here for fastening or striking the straps onto the belt. The order is compact and idiomatic: they affixed the armor‑straps to their waists.
segments.s264-g69: بر اسبان جنگی is a prepositional phrase “upon/onto war‑horses” (جنگی qualifying اسبان). سواران جنگ is an appositive/epithet “the horsemen/warriors of battle.” The sequence places the setting (on horses) before naming the actors (the riders).
segments.s264-g70: یکی here functions adverbially as “as one / together / at once.” برکشیدند is a past plural verb “they lifted/raised” (often used of drawing or raising lances/spears). چون introduces a simile “like/as,” and سنگ تنگ is the image — “a (close/solid) stone/rock” — so the line says they raised (their weapons) as one, like a compact rock. The poetic order emphasizes the simile.
segments.s264-g71: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” بر + بادپایان: بادپایان (plural form) means “the fleet‑footed ones” (here horses or swift riders) and بر indicates mounting/onto; ببستند (they bound/tied) is the verb — together giving “when they bound/fastened the saddle on the swift‑footed [horses].” زین is the direct object ‘saddle’ placed at the end for poetic effect.
segments.s264-g72: This is a paired descriptive phrase: پر از خشم گردان — گردان (troop) described as پر از خشم “full of anger” — and دل پر ز کین — the heart(s) described as پر ز کین “full of hatred/vengeance.” Note the slight variation in the preposition (از / ز) and the adjective‑like use of پر (“full of”), a common poetic compression linking the two coordinated images.
segments.s264-g9: بپوشید is a past tense (narrative) verb 'he put on/donned' placed before the subject (poetic inversion). هومان is the subject (proper name). سلیح نبرد is a noun phrase where نبرد ('battle') qualifies سلیح ('weapon/armament') — i.e., 'war‑gear' or 'weapons of battle'.
segments.s264-g10: سخن is the direct object 'the speech/words' placed up front for emphasis; پیش پیران is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the elders'. همه modifies سخن ('all the words') and یاد کرد is the compound expression 'made remembrance / recalled' (یاد + کرد = 'recalled, mentioned'). کرد is past tense 3rd‑singular.
segments.s264-g11: که introduces reported content ('that ...'). من is the explicit subject 'I'. بیژن گیو را is the definite/marked object (را) giving the person sought — here 'Bijan (of Giv)' in apposition/patronymic form. خواستم is past tense 'I wanted/sought'.
segments.s264-g12: همه شب 'all night' (an adverbial time phrase). همی is the archaic continuous particle meaning 'constantly/kept on'. جنگش is جنگ + enclitic 3sg 'his battle/war'. آراستم is first‑person past 'I adorned/prepared' — here 'I prepared/arrayed him for battle.'
segments.s264-g13: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / someone'. ترجمان را is the direct object 'the interpreter' (را marking definiteness). ز لشکر uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from' to indicate source. بخواند is past narrative 'he called/summoned'.
segments.s264-g14: به is the preposition 'onto / on'. گلگون is an adjective 'rosy / flower‑colored' modifying the following noun. بادآورش is a compound adjective/noun from بادآور 'wind‑bringing' (here describing the seat/cushion) — the phrase describes the object on which the interpreter was placed. برنشاند is past 'he caused to sit / seated (him)'.
segments.s264-g15: که introduces reported speech. رو (imperative 'go') here has a directional sense 'go toward / approach' (poetic usage). پیش بیژن = 'before/in the presence of Bijan'. بگویش is the verb بگو + enclitic ‑یش 'to him' meaning 'tell him'. که زود introduces the content 'that (he should come) quickly'.
segments.s264-g16: بیایی is a subjunctive/optative form 'that he come / if he come' used in reported commands to express 'come'. دمان‌گر is an epithet meaning an attendant/follower (here 'companion/attendant'), used appositionally. من آیم uses the present/subjunctive form آیم to mean 'I will come' in this context. چو دود is a simile 'like smoke' (چو = 'as/like', دود = 'smoke').
segments.s264-g145: فرو برد و کردش سر از تن جدا is a compact past action sequence. فرو برد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'drove/thrust (down/in)'; و links the two clauses; کردش is 'made/did to him/it' (here 'caused to be'), followed by the direct object سر 'head' and the prepositional phrase از تن جدا 'separate from the body' (از + تن + adjective جدا). The verb phrase precedes or accompanies its object in this poetic inversion.
segments.s264-g146: فگندش به سان یکی اژدها: فگندش is a past (they-)threw it / cast it; به سان is the fixed prepositional simile 'like / as'; یکی here functions as 'a certain / a' before the noun اژدها 'dragon'. The clause describes the manner in which the head (or object) was thrown.
segments.s264-g147: بغلتید هومان به خاک اندرون: بغلتید is a past intransitive verb 'rolled/tumbled' (poetic past ending -ید); the proper name هومان follows (verb–subject order is common in poetry). به خاک اندرون is an adverbial phrase 'to/into the ground / within the earth' (به + خاک + اندرون) specifying where he rolled.
segments.s264-g148: همه دشت شد سر به سر جوی خون: همه دشت is the subject 'the whole plain'; شد is the past copular/transformative 'became'; سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'all over / from head to head / throughout'; جوی خون is the complement 'a stream of blood'. Word order is Subject – Verb – Predicate phrase.
segments.s264-g149: نگه کرد بیژن بدان پیلتن: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction 'made a gaze' = 'looked'; in poetic order the verb phrase appears before the subject بیژن. بدان پیلتن is a locative/directional phrase ('to/at that Pīlten'); here the preposition ب- (ب = به) introduces the demonstrative reference (دان in the text is part of that demonstrative expression).
segments.s264-g150: فگنده چو سرو سهی بر چمن: فگنده is a past participial/passive 'thrown / cast / scattered'; چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; سرو سهی is the comparison noun phrase 'a (dark/poetic) cypress' (سهی is a poetic epithet modifying سرو); بر چمن is the locative 'upon the meadow'. The whole phrase modifies whatever was cast.
segments.s264-g151: شگفت آمدش سخت و برگشت از اوی: شگفت آمدش is an impersonal/experiential construction 'wonder came upon him' = 'he was amazed'; سخت is an intensifier 'greatly / severely'; و links to برگشت 'he turned/returned'; از اوی is 'from him' (اوی = archaic form of او 'him'). Verb forms are past, and the sentence uses classical/poetic pronoun forms.
segments.s264-g152: سوی کردگار جهان کرد روی: سوی + NP is the directional 'toward'; کردگار جهان is a genitival/title 'the Creator (of) the world' (کردگار modified by جهان); کرد روی is a light-verb construction 'made (his) face' = 'turned (his) face'. The clause expresses the act of turning one's face toward God.
segments.s264-g265: سزد‌گر is an evaluative phrase meaning “fitting / appropriate” (here introducing a conditional: “if you judge it fitting”). کنی is the second-person present/subjunctive (or jussive/imperative-like) form of کردن, used here to mean “you do / you undertake.” جنگ را uses را as the definite/object marker marking جنگ as the object. تیز چنگ is an adjective + noun phrase: تیز “sharp” modifies چنگ “claw” (here figurative for a fierce, sharp attack).
segments.s264-g266: به introduces purpose/benefit (here: “for / to bring about”). کین برادر is a genitival phrase “the vengeance of (or for) the brother” meaning “to avenge the brother.” نسازی is ساختن with the negative prefix ن- attached (classical/poetic) meaning “do not make / do not cause”; here it governs درنگ, the noun “delay,” so نسازی درنگ = “do not make a delay.”
segments.s264-g267: به ایرانیان marks the target/indirect object (“against the Iranians”); بر before شبیخون (شبیخون = “night-raid / surprise attack”) indicates the action directed upon them. کنی again is the second-person present/subjunctive/imperative-form of کردن, here continuing the conditional sense introduced earlier (“if you do/launch …”).
segments.s264-g268: زمین را uses را to mark زمین (“the land / earth”) as definite/object. به خون is resultative/transformative: “into/with blood” (describing the effect). رود جیحون is a noun phrase “the Jeyhun River” used as a simile/metaphor — the earth will be made like the Jeyhun in blood. کنی again repeats the verbal frame “you will make / cause.”
segments.s264-g269: ببر is the archaic/poetic imperative of برداشتن / گرفتن here: “take!” (2nd-person imperative). ده هزار is the numeral phrase (omitted from the listed words but present in the line) modifying آزموده سوار: آزموده “tried / experienced” modifies سوار “horseman / rider.” Word order is numeral + adjective + noun, typical in Persian epic poetry.
segments.s264-g270: کمر بسته is a participial/adjectival phrase literally “belt-bound” = “girded” (بسته from بستن). بر marks purpose/intent (“for / toward”); کینه و کارزار is the coordinated purpose: کینه “vengeance / grudge” and کارزار “battle / combat.” Together: they are girded/ready for vengeance and fighting.
segments.s264-g271: مگر here functions adversatively/contrastively (“but rather” / “rather”), introducing an exhortation. کین هومان is a possessive/genitival phrase “Homan’s vengeance” or “the vengeance for Homan”; تو is the emphatic pronoun “you” placed before the verb for emphasis. بازآوری (باز + آوری) is a verbal noun/verb “bring back / restore” used as the main action directed at کین هومان (“restore/bring back vengeance”).
segments.s264-g272: سر دشمنان را marks “the enemies’ heads” as the definite object with را. به here indicates means or manner (“by / with”); گاز in this context is figurative/archaic meaning “a bite / a seize / a grip” (i.e., to seize violently). آوری (from آوردن) is second-person present/subjunctive/imperative “you bring / you cause to be brought,” so به گاز آوری = “you bring (them) by seizing” = “seize the enemies’ heads.”
segments.s264-g73: کمانها is the plural subject ('the bows'). چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). بایست is an archaic/poetic intransitive verb here meaning 'stood/was set' and precedes the main past verb. برخاستند is the main past tense verb (3rd pl.) 'they rose/rose up', agreeing with the plural subject; the clause as a whole means 'when the bows were set/stood up'.
segments.s264-g74: به میدان تنگ اندرون is a directional prepositional phrase: به + میدان (arena/field) qualified by the adjective تنگ ('narrow') and the locative اندرون ('inner/within'). تاختند is the past plural verb 'they rushed/charged' and continues the narrative action begun in the previous clause; word order places the direction before the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s264-g75: چپ و راست are directional adverbs ('left and right'). گردان and پیچان are participial/adjectival forms ('turning, revolving' and 'twisting') describing action on the following noun. عنان ('reins') is the head noun; together the phrase means 'turning and twisting the reins to left and right.'
segments.s264-g76: همان is a demonstrative ('the same' / 'those same') qualifying the following nouns. نیزه and سنان are weapons ('spear' and here 'spear' again), while آب‌داده is an adjectival compound 'water‑given' or 'water‑wet' modifying سنان; the phrase lists their spears/spearpoints. The construction is a poetic noun phrase rather than a full clause.
segments.s264-g77: زرهشان is the possessed noun 'their armor'. در آورد شد is a compound verbal sequence (در + آورد + شد) functioning as a passive/resultative 'was taken off' (درآوردن 'to take off' with شد as auxiliary). لخت لخت is reduplicated adjective/adverb ('bare/naked, strip by strip') emphasizing the state after removal.
segments.s264-g78: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'see'. تا introduces the subordinate clause of extent/time ('until' / 'see to the point of'), followed by the archaic interrogative کرا ('who/whom'). روز برگشت و بخت is the predicate 'the day returned and (the) fortune (returned)' with برگشت as the past verb applying to both روز and بخت; word order is flexible and poetic, with object/affected nouns preceding the verb.
segments.s264-g79: دهنشان is the possessed noun 'their mouths'. همی is an ongoing/durative adverb ('still'). از تبش gives the cause/agentive phrase ('from its heat/fever' or 'because of its ardour'). مانده is the past participle/verb 'remained', and باز here is the adjective 'open' (so 'remained open').
segments.s264-g80: به آب و به آسایش is a paired prepositional phrase ('for water and for rest/comfort') indicating the object/aim of need. آمد is the past verb 'came/arose' and نیاز is the noun subject 'need' (so literally 'a need came for water and for rest'). Note the inverted order (verb + noun) common in Persian.
segments.s264-g209: به + place + رسید is a directional verb phrase: به توران رسید = 'reached (into) Turan'. آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' that follows the verb cluster here, and ترجمان is the subject placed after the time phrase (classical Persian allows this flexible order). The main verb رسید is simple past, 3rd person singular.
segments.s264-g210: بگفت is the past-tense report verb 'he said/said of it'; آنچ دید is a fused relative/wh-clause 'that which he saw' (آنچ = 'what/that which', دید = 'saw'). از بد بدگمان is an adverbial phrase of cause/manner: از marks 'from/out of' and بدگمان ('ill-suspicious') characterises the speaker's biased/suspicious attitude, with بد intensifying the negativity.
segments.s264-g211: هم آنگه is a temporal/discourse marker 'at that same moment/then also'; به پیران رسید آگهی literally 'news reached the elders' (به marks the indirect object 'to the elders'; رسید is past 'reached'; آگهی is the thing that arrived = 'news/information').
segments.s264-g212: که introduces a content clause 'that ...' and شد is past 'became'. تیره is a predicative adjective 'dark/ominous' describing the state of the following noun phrase آن فر شاهنشهی ('that farr/royal glory of kingship'), which together form the clause 'that the royal glory became dark.'
segments.s264-g213: سبک precedes the name and functions adverbially/adjectivally here: 'light' or 'swift', describing بیژن's manner. اندر میان سپاه is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the army/among the ranks'. Word order places the manner/adjective before the person and the location after.
segments.s264-g214: نگونسار کرد is a causative construction: نگونسار ('mournful') is the resultative adjective and کرد ('made/did') is the past-tense verb 'made' — together 'made (it) mournful'. آن درفش سیاه is the direct object 'that black banner/standard'. The verb precedes the object in this poetic ordering.
segments.s264-g215: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن دیده‌بانان ایران سپاه is a nominal group 'those lookouts/sentinels of the Iranian army' (آن = 'those/the', دیده‌بانان = 'lookouts', ایران سپاه = 'Iranian army' in juxtaposed genitive style).
segments.s264-g216: نگون یافتند combines نگون ('fallen/overthrown/ill-fated') placed before the verb with یافتند ('they found') in the past plural, so 'they found (it) fallen/ill-fated.' آن درفش سیاه is the object 'that black standard.'
segments.s264-g137: ز is the poetic preposition (like modern از) introducing the means/agent: 'with/through every kind of force.' The structure is [preposition + NP] + verb: ز هر گونه زور آزمودند = 'they tried/tested every kind of force.' The conjunction و then attaches the next noun بند as the subject/topic of the following clause (inversion is common in epic Persian).
segments.s264-g138: فراز آمد is a verbal/adverbial phrase meaning 'rose aloft' (فراز = 'on high'). آن بند is the demonstrative + noun 'that bond/fetter' serving as the subject/topic; چرخ بلند is a post‑modifier identifying the bond as belonging to the lofty sky/heavens ('the lofty sky'). Word order is slightly inverted: verb (فراز آمد) + subject (آن بند) + modifier (چرخ بلند).
segments.s264-g139: بزد is the simple past 3rd‑person 'he struck.' دست here names the instrument ('with his hand') and is placed after the verb in poetic/inverted order (more common than colloquial Persian). بیژن is the agent/topic; به سان پلنگ is a simile 'like a leopard' (به + سان = 'in the manner of').
segments.s264-g140: ز ... تا ... is a range expression 'from X to Y' (ز = from, تا = as far as). میانش = میان + enclitic ـش 'his' = 'his mid‑section.' The verb بیازید follows the range phrase, with چنگ ('claw') as the affected/agentive noun: the construction means the claw tore/gaped across that span. Inversion (verb before its noun) is poetic but common; here بیازید expresses 'gaped/ripped/opened.'
segments.s264-g141: گرفتش is گرفت + enclitic ـش 'him' = 'he seized him.' The preposition به introduces the loci of the grip ('by/at'): به چپ گردن و راست ران = 'by the left (side of the) neck and the right thigh.' The coordinate structure uses و to link the two body‑locations. Note the adjective-like words چپ/راست specify side and precede the body noun in this poetic order.
segments.s264-g142: خم آورد is a compound verbal expression: خم (bend) + آورد (bring) = 'caused to bend' or 'bent.' پشت هیون گران is a noun phrase 'the back of the heavy beast' (هیون = beast/monster, گران = heavy) and is the object whose back is bent. Word order places the verb before its object in an elevated, poetic style.
segments.s264-g143: برآوردش is برآورد + enclitic ـش 'him' — here a past action 'he lifted/raised him' (برآوردن can mean 'raise/lift' in epic usage). از جای = 'from his place.' The conjunction و links a second verb بنهاد (past of بنهادن 'to place/set'), so و بنهاد پست = 'and set him low/laid him low.' The two verbs form a sequence of actions on the same object (ـش).
segments.s264-g144: سوی خنجر آورد = 'brought (it/his hand) toward the dagger' (سوی = toward(s); آورد = 'brought'). چون باد دست is a simile 'his hand like the wind' meaning he moved his hand swiftly like the wind. The clause thus describes a quick, wind‑like bringing of the hand toward the dagger.
segments.s264-g185: بترسید is a plural/formal imperative (or hortative) meaning “fear/guard against.” از introduces the object of fear. انبوه functions as a noun/adjective meaning a large crowd or multitude. مردم is the substantive “people.” کشان is a deverbal/participial adjectival element (“‑dragging,” “those who drag”), attached to مردم to describe what kind of people are meant.
segments.s264-g186: که here introduces a relative/explanatory clause (“who/for/for they”). یابند is 3rd‑person plural (present/subjunctive/epic present) of ‘to find’ — here “they will find” / “they find.” ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from.” آن + کار = “that deed/act.” یکسر is an adverb meaning “entirely/utterly.” نشان means “sign/trace.” The clause therefore says what those people will (not) find regarding that deed.
segments.s264-g187: به + جنگ + اندر + آیند is the idiomatic sequence “enter into battle” (به ... اندر آیند = they go/come into the fight). آیند is 3rd‑plural ‘they come/enter.’ بر سان کوه is a simile: سان = “like/as,” with بر giving the sense “upon/like,” so “like a mountain.” Word order places the directional/verb cluster before the simile.
segments.s264-g188: بسنده نباشد is a negative predication: بسنده = “sufficient/enough,” نباشد = “is not / will not remain” (3rd‑person). مگر = “except / unless.” با گروه = “with a group/company/ troop.” Together: “It will not be sufficient except with a group,” i.e. one man alone will not suffice.
segments.s264-g189: برآهخت is an epic/past form meaning “he raised/removed” (a past action). درعِ سیاوش is the construct “Siavash’s armor,” and ز سر literally “from (his) head.” Word order places the object/possessive phrase before the source phrase z سر.
segments.s264-g190: به marks the direction/target (“onto/onto the person”). خفتان is a participial/adjectival form meaning “sleeping” (literally ‘one who sleeps’), modifying هومان (a proper name). بپوشید here is a perfective/past action meaning “he put on / clothed / covered” (3rd‑singular). بر at the end reinforces the notion of ‘on’ or ‘upon.’ Overall: “he put it on the sleeping Homan.”
segments.s264-g191: بر آن چرمه پیل‌پیکر نشست = “he sat on that hide (چرمه) that was elephant‑shaped (پیل‌پیکر).” بر = “on/upon”; آن points to the previously mentioned object; چرمه is a hide/skin (here a saddle or covering); پیل‌پیکر is a postpositive adjective meaning “elephant‑bodied/‑shaped.” نشست is the past “sat/settled.”
segments.s264-g192: درفش سر نامداران به دست is a nominal phrase describing what he holds: درفش = “banner/standard,” سر نامداران = “the chief/head (سر) of the famed/renowned (نامداران)” (i.e. the principal standard of the famed), and به دست = “in (his) hand” (به + instrument/role = by/with; دست = hand). Word order is nominal, listing the object being held and the means/location.
segments.s264-g153: که‌ای is an archaic/vocative-relative combination (که + ای) addressing the addressee: “you who …”. برتر + از forms a comparative: adjective برتر ‘higher/superior’ followed by the comparator از + noun (جایگاه و زمان) = “higher than place and time”. The coordinating و links جایگاه and زمان as two parallel nouns compared to the subject.
segments.s264-g154: ز here is the poetic ablative/genitive meaning “from” or “out of”; ز جان سخن‌گوی is a nominal phrase ‘speaker from the soul’ where سخن‌گوی (‘one who speaks’) functions as a substantive epithet. روشن‌روان is a compound adjective ‘bright‑soul(ed)/clear‑minded’ joined by و to the previous epithet, yielding two descriptive epithets of the addressee.
segments.s264-g155: توی تو is an emphatic locative/vocative expression (توی = ‘in/among’ + تو) addressing the person; که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. جز تو جهاندار نیست is literal ‘there is no world‑ruler except you’: جز + noun = ‘except’, and the negative copula نیست is placed at the end as usual.
segments.s264-g156: خرد را topicalizes or foregrounds ‘wisdom’ (را as definite/object/topic marker = ‘as for wisdom’). بدین is the fused prepositional phrase به + این ‘in/for this’, followed by کار ‘matter/affair’. پیکار نیست is the predicate ‘there is no fight/contest’ with the negative copula نیست at the end; overall: ‘As for wisdom, in this affair there is no contest.’
segments.s264-g157: مرا ... نیست is the existential/possessive pattern meaning ‘for me there is not …’ → ‘I do not have …’. ز این هنر = ‘in/from this art/skill’; سر به سر is an idiom meaning ‘utterly/completely’ (literally ‘head to head’). بهره means ‘share/portion’. The clause reads ‘I have absolutely no share in this art.’
segments.s264-g158: که introduces an explanatory or concessive clause. با پیل کین is an instrumental/genitive cluster ‘with an elephant(’s) vengeance’ (با = ‘with’, followed by the noun phrase پیل کین). جستنم is the infinitive/verb form ‘to seek’ (here conceptually ‘to attempt / my attempting’). زهره (here not matching the provided senses) is used idiomatically (likely ‘fortune/temperament’ or a personal/astrological reference) and نیست at the end negates the clause: ‘(I am) not one to, with an elephant’s vengeance, undertake the pursuit.’
segments.s264-g159: به کین سیاوش is a prepositional phrase ‘for the vengeance of Siawush / to avenge Siawush’ (به marking purpose/atonement). بریدمش is the past tense verb بریدم with the enclitic object ‑ش ‘him’ = ‘I cut him (off)’; سر (head) is the direct object / complement specifying what was cut. Word order is flexible in poetry, so the verb + clitic + object sequence yields ‘I severed his head for Siawush’s vengeance.’
segments.s264-g160: به هفتاد خون is a quantifying/prepositional phrase ‘by/for seventy (units of) blood’ (هفتاد = seventy, خون = blood/bloodshed) indicating the scale of vengeance; the following noun phrase برادر پدر is a genitive/attributive sequence literally ‘brother of the father’ (i.e. ‘father’s brother’). Together the phrase gives the measure/target of the revenge (‘seventyfold blood of the father’s brother’), a compressed poetic genitive construction.
segments.s264-g257: گشادی ('opening, a breaking-open') is a noun that here names the event affecting سپه ('the host/army'); سپه را uses را as the definite/object marker so the phrase means ‘the opening/rupture of the host’. بدین is the fused prepositional phrase ‘به + دین’ (poetic/archaic ‘with/ in this’), followed by جنگ ‘battle’ to give 'in this battle'. دست at the end of the segment functions as a noun meaning 'power/hand' (control, figuratively referring to the action or effect) linked to what follows.
segments.s264-g258: This clause gives the effect on a person: دل ('heart') is the patient/theme, qualified by شاه ترکان ('the king of the Turks' — شاه governs the genitive relationship to ترکان). به هم is an adverbial phrase meaning 'together' or 'into pieces' modifying the verb phrase, بر + شکست are an older poetic combination meaning 'broke' / 'was broken' (شکست is the past noun/verb 'break/defeat'). The whole means the Turk king's heart was broken to pieces.
segments.s264-g259: همه is a quantifier 'the whole/all' modifying لشکر ('host/army'); شاه ایران is a possessive/genitive sequence 'the King of Iran' modifying لشکر. چو is the comparative/simile 'like', and شیر is the comparison term 'lion' — so 'the whole host of the King of Iran, like a lion'.
segments.s264-g260: This sketched descriptive pair gives qualities of the simile: دمان (here a poetic/archaic word for the flowing skirt/hem or mane-like flourish) and دنان (an obscure/archaic poetic word) are joined by و. بادپایان (from بادپا) is an adjectival epithet 'fleet-footed/quick of foot'. به زیر is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the ground' or 'low', indicating the result or posture. Together the segment paints the army as lion-like, maned and swift-footed, brought low (or casting down).
segments.s264-g261: وز here is an archaic connective (classically a contraction of و + ز) meaning 'and from' or 'by virtue of'; اندوه پیران is a genitive phrase 'the sorrow/grief of the elders'. برآورد is a past-tense/perfective verb 'brought forth/caused/raised up' (here 'arose' or 'produced'), and خشم is the direct object 'anger' — 'from the elders' sorrow anger arose'.
segments.s264-g262: This is a descriptive clause about the heart: دل 'the heart' + از درد خسته 'weary from pain' (از introduces the cause, درد = emotional/physical pain, خسته = tired/weary). پر از 'full of' is an adjectival phrase and آب چشم literally 'water of the eye' = 'tears'. Grammatically the sequence describes state/result: the heart, exhausted by pain, was full of tears.
segments.s264-g263: به is the preposition 'to', نستیهن is a proper name (the addressee), آنگه is the temporal adverb 'then', فرستاد is the past-tense verb 'he sent', and کس is an indefinite noun 'someone' — 'then he sent someone to Nestiḥan.'
segments.s264-g264: This is the quoted or reported address. که‌ای here is a vocative/attention particle roughly 'that, O' (classical/poetic usage for calling), نامور is an epithet 'renowned/famed', گرد is the old imperative/verb 'become/turn into' (from گردیدن), and فریادرس is a compound noun 'rescuer/succorer' — together: 'O famed one, become a rescuer!'
segments.s264-g113: همی is an adverb of continued action meaning 'kept on' or 'still' placed before the verb. رزم جستند is a poetic verb phrase: رزم 'battle' plus جستند (past plural of جستن) here meaning 'launched into / engaged in' (literary) — together 'they engaged in battle'. یک با دگر (lit. 'one with other') is a reciprocal phrase 'one with the other / one another' following the verb and showing who fought whom.
segments.s264-g114: یکی را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker and here means 'not a single one / anyone' (in negative contexts). ز کینه gives the reason ('from / out of hatred'). نه negates the following verbal phrase برگشت سر: برگشت is past 'turned/returned' and سر 'head' is its object, so برگشت سر = 'turned (one's) head'; together the clause means 'not one turned his head because of hatred' (i.e. no one turned away out of enmity).
segments.s264-g115: This is a verbless descriptive phrase listing two conditions. دهن خشک = 'mouth dry' (noun + adjective). و links the next description. غرقه شده تن در آب is 'the body having been soaked in water': غرقه شده is a perfect participle 'become drenched' modifying تن 'body', and در آب gives the location 'in water'. The construction is typical of poetic compression (nouns followed by modifiers/participles).
segments.s264-g116: از (here) introduces cause: 'from / because of'. آن رنج = 'that suffering' (referring back to prior hardship). و links another cause. تابیدن آفتاب is a gerundive/infinitive construction 'the shining of the sun' where تابیدن ('to shine') nominalizes and آفتاب ('sun') supplies the agent; together they complete the reason 'from that suffering and the sun's shining.'
segments.s264-g117: وز آن پس is a classical sequence meaning 'after that / thenceforth' (وز here is an archaic variant of از). به + دستوری functions instrumentally: به دستوری 'by an order / by command' (دستوری is 'imperative/commanding'). یکدگر is reciprocal 'one another' and indicates that the command/order was mutual or applied to them collectively. (Note: وز is archaic here; if the precise dictionary senses above don't include 'از', use -1.)
segments.s264-g118: برفتند is past plural 'they went / they departed'. پویان is adverbial/participle meaning 'moving/hastening' and modifies the verb, so 'they went hastening' or 'they hurried off'. سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)' and آبخور is the destination 'watering-place'. The verb + adverbial + prepositional phrase yields 'they hastened toward the watering-place.'
segments.s264-g119: بخورد آب is a compact verb-object clause: بخورد (past of خوردن with the prefix ب‑ for poetic ordering) = 'he drank' and آب = 'water'. و links clauses. برخاست بیژن is 'Bijan rose / stood up' (برخاست past). به درد expresses his state 'with / in pain' (به used to indicate condition/result). The implied subject of the first verb is carried over; the explicit name بیژن appears with the second verb.
segments.s264-g120: ز introduces source/relation 'of / from' here in a possessive/partitive sense. دادار is the Creator/God. نیکی دهش is a compound: نیکی 'goodness' and دهش 'giving/grant' (together either 'the giver of goodness' or 'the gift of goodness'); یاد کرد is the verb 'he remembered' (literally 'did remembrance'). The whole line says he remembered the Creator's kindly gift (i.e. he recalled the beneficence of God).
segments.s264-g81: پس is a discourse adverb meaning 'then/so'. آسوده گشتند is a past-tense, plural verb phrase: گشتند (they became) with the predicate adjective آسوده (at ease/relieved) — 'they became relieved'. و links the two coordinated past actions. دم برزدند is literal 'they struck/beat breath' where دم (breath) is the object and برزدند (past plural) conveys giving/letting out a breath or cry; idiomatically 'they drew a breath' or 'they exhaled/uttered a sound.'
segments.s264-g82: بر آن آتش تیز is a prepositional phrase 'upon that fierce/keen fire' where بر governs the noun phrase آن آتش modified by the adjective تیز (here qualifying آتش). نم برزدند follows as a verb phrase: نم (moisture, drops) is the thing they 'struck' or dashed, and برزدند is again past plural meaning 'they struck/beat' — together 'they dashed drops of moisture upon that fierce fire.'
segments.s264-g83: سپر برگرفتند is a past plural clause: برگرفتند (they took up) with سپر (shields) as object. The following و شمشیر تیز is a coordination where the verb (برگرفتند) is understood to apply also to the noun phrase 'شمشیر تیز' (sharp swords); this is a common ellipsis in Persian poetry (verb is omitted the second time but implied).
segments.s264-g84: برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose' or 'issued forth'. It takes the following verbal noun phrase خروشیدن ('the roaring/crying') as its subject/obj‑like complement; together برآمد خروشیدن means 'a roar arose'. رستخیز (an uprising) is the genitive/qualifier of خروشیدن (the roar of the uprising), so the whole phrase reads 'the roar of an uprising arose.'
segments.s264-g85: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. برق درفشان is a compound image: برق (lightning) + درفشان (shining/flash­ing) — 'flashing lightning'. که here is a relative/linking particle 'that' introducing the subordinate clause which describes the lightning-image: از تیره میغ = 'from the dark/black of the blade' (از + تیره + میغ), where تیره modifies the noun میغ (blade/edge).
segments.s264-g86: همی is a classical/prosodic particle often prefacing verbs in epic Persian to give an emphatic or continuous sense; here it precedes the past verb افروخت (kindled) — 'it kindled/was kindling'. افروخت is perfective past 'kindled'. از هر دو تیغ is a prepositional phrase 'from/between both blades' (هر here works with the implied dual 'both'); syntactically it indicates the source or location of the sparks/fire — 'fire kindled from both swords.'
segments.s264-g87: ز آهن ... reads as 'from iron/blade to ...' where ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بدان is here the prepositional compound 'to that' made of ب (to/into) + دان (an archaic/demonstrative element equivalent to 'that') — an older poetic formation; thus 'ز آهن بدان آهن آبدار' = 'from iron to that iron (آبدار).' آهن repeats the noun (blade/iron) and آبدار is an adjective 'moist' or 'water‑rich/tempered' modifying the second آهن.
segments.s264-g88: نیامد is past negative 'did not come'. The remainder 'به زخم اندرون تابدار' is the complement: به (to/into) + زخم (wound) + اندرون (the inner parts/inside). تابدار is an adjective 'resilient/enduring' (i.e., lasting). Together: 'no lasting wound came into the inner parts.'
