segments.s264-g177: بدو گفت بیژن مترس از گزند is reported speech with an inverted word order common in classical Persian. بدو is an allative/enclitic pronoun meaning 'to him' placed before the reporting verb گفت (past 3rd sg. of گفتن, 'said'). بیژن appears after گفت as the explicit subject for emphasis. مترس is a negative imperative meaning 'do not fear' — an older/poetic form equivalent to modern نترس — followed by the prepositional phrase از گزند 'from harm'.
segments.s264-g178: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason/explanation ('that/for'). پیمان همان است is a verbless/state clause where پیمان 'the covenant/pledge' is commented on by the demonstrative/adverb همان 'the same' and linked by the copula است 'is', indicating the agreement still holds. و بگشاد بند contains the past verb بگشاد (classical past 3rd sg. with the prefixed ب- common in older Persian, 'he opened/loosed') with an omitted subject that is recovered from context (Bijan or the mentioned agent), and بند as its direct object 'bond/fetter'. Word order and omission of the subject are typical of poetic narrative.
segments.s264-g179: تو اکنون سوی لشکر خویش پوی has a fairly direct word order: تو 'you' (subject), اکنون 'now' (temporal adverb), سوی + لشکر خویش 'toward your army' (prepositional phrase), and پوی is an imperative/command 'go; set out' (classical/poetic verbal form). خویش + ش is the possessive/reflexive element indicating 'one's own' (here functioning as 'your').
segments.s264-g180: ز من هرچ دیدی بدیشان بگوی is a sequence: ز من 'concerning/from me' marks the source, هرچ دیدی 'whatever you saw' (هرچ = 'whatever' + دیدی past 'you saw'), and بدیشان بگوی is an imperative 'tell them' (بدیشان = به ایشان 'to them', بگوی = tell/say). The clause thus instructs the addressee to report all they observed about the speaker.
segments.s264-g181: بشد here functions as a past/resultative 'became'/'it came to pass', followed by the nominal phrase ترجمان بیژن 'Bijan [as] the translator/spokesman' (a predicative complement). آمد is the simple past 'came' and دمان (دامان) is a locative 'foothill/edge' — together: 'Bijan became the spokesman and came to the foot (of the mountain).' The verb-first بشد is typical of epic/poetic inversion.
segments.s264-g182: به کوه کنابد به زه بر کمان describes actions at the mountain: به کوه is a locative/directional 'to the mountain' (or 'at the mountain'), کنابد is a classical present/past-form conveying effort/straining (here best rendered 'strung/strained'), and به زه بر کمان indicates the object/instrument: 'the string onto the bow' (به = with/onto, زه = bowstring, بر = on/upon, کمان = bow). So the clause means he strung the bowstring on the bow at the mountain.
segments.s264-g183: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نگه کرد is a light-verb construction where نگه 'a look/gaze' combines with کرد 'did/made' to mean 'looked' or 'cast a gaze'. ز آن رزمگاه 'from that battlefield' gives the vantage point. The whole segment reads 'When Bijan looked from that battlefield...'.
segments.s264-g184: نبودش گذر جز به توران سپاه is a negative-existential construction: نبودش 'there was not' (with the clitic ش referring to 'it/there'), followed by گذر 'a passage/way'. جز به توران سپاه 'except to/through the army of Turan' restricts the only possible passage. Put together: 'there was no passage except through the Turanian host.'
segments.s264-g33: که here introduces the reported/speech clause ('who' / 'that'). با + گفت functions as 'said to' (classical Persian often uses با with گفت to mark the addressee). آهوان is the indirect object 'the gazelles' (plural). غرم ژیان is a terse, poetic phrase attached to the speech: غرم = 'my/one's wrath' or 'in wrath' and ژیان = 'life' (used vocatively or as an exclamatory nominal phrase within the quotation).
segments.s264-g34: که‌گر is the conditional 'if'. دشت (subject) + گردد (present/subjunctive of گشت 'become') gives 'if the plain becomes' (subjunctive used after the conditional). همه modifies پرنیان: 'all / entirely silken' or 'becomes all of silk' (پرنیان = silken cloth / silken).
segments.s264-g35: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' / 'out of'. دامی = 'a snare / trap'. که introduces the relative clause modifying دامی. پای من آزاد گشت = 'my foot became free' (پای 'foot' = figuratively 'one's step/ability to move'; آزاد 'free'; گشت past 'became'). The whole phrase gives an ablative cause: 'from the snare from which my foot was freed.'
segments.s264-g36: نپویم is a first‑person negative form meaning 'I will not go / I do not go' (negative present/volitional used for refusal). بر ... سوی = 'toward / to the direction of'; آن سوی آباد دشت = 'that side / that quarter of the inhabited/prosperous plain'. Word order places the directional phrase after the verb as is normal in Persian.
segments.s264-g37: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic sequence meaning 'thus gave answer / thus replied' (چنین 'thus'; داد 'gave' used as auxiliary for 'gave an answer'; پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the content of the reply. امروز here is temporal 'today / for the present' modifying the following proper name گیو.
segments.s264-g38: بماند is a jussive/optative or present-tense meaning 'shall/remain / let (him) remain'. جگر is used figuratively here as 'heart / seat of feeling' (poetic for 'heart' rather than the anatomical liver). خسته qualifies it: 'weary / spent.' بر + (name) marks the object/position: 'upon / on Pour‑Niyu' — پور نیو functions as a name (Pour‑Niyu) in the poem. The phrase states that Giv will remain while his weary heart rests with/for Pour‑Niyu.
segments.s264-g39: به + چنگ = 'into / in the grip / in the talon'. منی ی (منی + ی) is an archaic/poetic predicative construction meaning 'it is mine / in my hand' (ی is an enclitic copula/existential element). در here functions as 'in / inside' (reinforcing the sense of being held). به سان = 'like / as'. تذرو is a verb/noun here meaning 'to scatter' or a name/term used metaphorically; the phrase as a whole compares the thing-in-hand to something scattered or like a small bird thrown about.
segments.s264-g40: که introduces a relative clause describing the preceding simile: 'which ...'. بازش = باز + -ش enclitic object; here باز is 'the hawk' (classical Persian باز = 'hawk') and -ش = 'it/him/her' (object clitic). برد is the verb 'took / carried away' (past). بر سر = 'to / onto the top of'; شاخ سرو = 'the bough/branch of the cypress' (شاخ 'branch', سرو 'cypress').
segments.s264-g97: 'بدین گونه' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in this manner' (literally built from the prefix ب + دین + گونه here functioning as the frozen phrase 'بدین گونه'); 'جستند' is 3rd-person plural past of جستن but in epic usage here means 'they sought' (not literal 'leapt'), and it takes the following noun phrase 'ننگ و نبرد' as its object. Word order: adverbial phrase → verb → object. The verb ending -ند marks plural past/habitual action.
segments.s264-g98: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ('so that' or 'with the result that'); 'از پشت زین' is a compound prepositional phrase (از = 'from' + پشت زین = 'behind the saddle'); اندر is an older preposition/particle 'into/in' used here as part of the verbal phrase; 'آرند' is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive of آوردن ('they bring/drag'), used to express the resulting action in the clause; 'مرد' is the direct object 'the man/hero'.
segments.s264-g99: The noun phrase 'کمربند گیرد' contains کمربند ('girdle/belt') as object and گیرد (3rd-singular present of گرفتن) meaning 'grasps' or 'seizes'. کرا is an archaic relative pronoun 'who'. زور بیش is a postpositive descriptive phrase 'greater strength' or 'with excess strength' — here qualifying the one who seizes the belt.
segments.s264-g100: رباید is an archaic/persuasive-present form meaning 'will snatch/seize' (3rd-person); it functions as the main action verb. 'ز اسب افگند' is a light-verb sequence meaning 'threw/caused (him) from the horse' (ز = 'from', اسب = 'horse', افگند = 'threw/cast'); grammatically the two verbs coordinate to show seizure followed by casting down. 'خوار پیش' is an adverbial phrase — خوار 'low/despised' and پیش 'before/in front of' — giving the manner/result ('cast down in disgrace before others').
segments.s264-g101: ز is the poetic preposition here meaning 'because of' and introduces the cause. نیروی گردان is a genitive phrase 'the force/strength of the troop' (نیروی + گردان, with the possessive/attributive relation implicit). دوال رکیب follows as a postposed modifier: دوال 'twofold/pair' + رکیب 'rider/companion' — together 'the two riders' (adjectival phrase after the noun as is normal in Persian poetry).
segments.s264-g102: گسست is the past/inchoative sense of گسستن used as a simple past intransitive verb 'broke/separated' (here rendered 'were torn apart'). اندر is the locative preposition 'in/within' and آوردگاه is the place 'battlefield'. از is used instrumentally/causally 'by/from' and نهیب is a loud/thunderous cry; together از نهیب means 'by the shout' or 'because of the cry.'
segments.s264-g103: همیدون is treated here as a proper name (vocative/subject). نگشتند is the past negative plural of گشتن 'to turn/leave/turn away' — 'they did not turn/leave.' ز before اسبان is the ablative 'from' and اسبان 'horses'; جدا is an adverb/adjective 'apart' — together 'did not separate from the horses.' The negative applies to the following locative/separative phrase.
segments.s264-g104: نبودند is the past negative of بودن 'they were not'. بر یکدگر is a prepositional phrase 'upon one another/against each other' (بر = on/upon; یکدگر = one another). پادشا is an archaic/formal vocative 'O king' placed at the end to address the ruler; syntactically the clause states they 'were not upon one another, O king.'
segments.s264-g41: خروشان and خون are coordinated visual/auditory elements: خروشان is a participial/adjectival form meaning "roaring" and stands before و خون as a kind of headline description. از + دیده marks the source/point of origin ("from the eyes"). چکان is a present/stative verbal form (poetic) meaning "dripping"; the clause literally reads "roaring and blood from two eyes dripping," i.e. "with a roar, blood dripped from his two eyes."
segments.s264-g42: کشانش is the deverbal stem کشان with the pronominal suffix ‑ش ("him/it") and functions as a verbal clause meaning "(they) dragged him" or "dragging him." به چنگال is an instrumental/agentive phrase here ("by the talons"). The second coordination و خونش مکان is elliptical: خونش "his blood" plus مکان "place/ground" implies a copula or result ("and his blood [was] the place/ground" → "his blood stained the ground").
segments.s264-g43: بدو گفت بیژن is an inverted indirect-object-before-verb sequence: بدو "to him" + گفت (here the verb "said") + بیژن (the speaker). که introduces the following clause (complement or indirect question). تا کی سخن is an interrogative phrase: تا کی "until when/how long" and سخن "speech/talking" — together "how long will you speak?" Note: the annotated senses list does not include the verb-sense 'to say' for گفت, so the semantic sense is given as a speech-verb in the explanation.
segments.s264-g44: کجا is the interrogative "where." خواهی is the second-person present/future/volitional marker ("will you / do you intend"). آهنگ here means "aim/intention" and is the object of آورد (آوردن "to bring/produce/make"). The final کن is an emphatic/imperative light verb attached to آورد (a periphrastic imperative "bring/do [it]" → "make your move"), so the clause reads "Where will you direct your aim? — bring it about / act."
segments.s264-g45: به کوه is a directional prepositional phrase "to the mountain." کنابد is an archaic/narrative-present verb form (third‑person present in style) meaning roughly "he/one toils/acts"; here it functions as an exhortation or narrative instruction. کنی is the verbal form (subjunctive/second person) meaning "you should do / you do" and takes کارزار "battle/war" as its object: together "(go) to the mountain, make/do battle."
segments.s264-g46: اگر introduces a conditional "if." سوی is a directional noun "toward/that direction." زیبد is a present-tense/optative verb meaning "it befits / becomes proper" ("if it befits to turn that way"). برآرای is an imperative/instructional verb from برآرایی/برآراختن meaning "array/prepare/arrange," and کار is the object "affair/operation (here: the military action)." The whole clause therefore reads, "if it befits to turn that way, arrange/prepare the action."
segments.s264-g47: که introduces a reason/explanation clause ("for/that"). فریادرسمان is a compound noun meaning "rescuer/one who answers cries for help." نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive of بودن ("there is not / will not be"); z + دور (ز دور) is a poetic ablative phrase meaning "from afar." Together: "for no rescuer will be near from afar."
segments.s264-g48: The correlative negative construction نه ... نه ... lists who will not help: ایران گراید به یاری نه تور. گراید is a present/future-form of گراییدن "to turn/ incline" and here means "will turn/come (to help)." به یاری is the infinitival/advantage phrase "to help/for help." تور is a proper name (Tūr) contrasted with Iran; the clause means "neither Iran will turn to help, nor Tur."
segments.s264-g25: زره is the head noun 'armor'. با گره is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with knots' that modifies زره. The following بر بر پهلوی is a prepositional phrase 'on (the) flank/side' — the doubled بر is a poetic repetition/metrical feature but both function as the preposition 'on/upon' heading پهلوی 'flank'. Word order (noun + modifier + prepositional phrase) is typical but the double بر is literary.
segments.s264-g26: درفشان is an epithet 'shining/glittering' modifying سر. Here the adjective-like form precedes the noun (poetic inversion) to create 'the shining head'. از introduces the source 'from' and مغفر خسروی is a genitival phrase 'the royal covering/veil' (مغفر as covering, خسروی 'royal'), i.e. the head is gleaming because of/under a royal covering.
segments.s264-g27: به هومان is a prepositional phrase 'to/for Homan' (indirect object). چنین گفت is 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' — poetic past). کای is the vocative 'O', and بادسار is an epithet 'wind-bringer' used as the form of address. The phrase is a direct speech introducer.
segments.s264-g28: ببردی is a past 2nd-person form 'you took/carried away'. ز من 'from me' gives the source/possessor, with من functioning as object/possessive. دوش is a temporal adverb 'last night' placed before the thing taken, and سر یاددار is the direct object 'the keepsake/memento of the head' (a compound-like phrase). Overall: 'you took from me last night the head-memento.'
segments.s264-g29: امیدستم is the past tense 'I hoped/expected'. امروز is the temporal adverb 'today'. کین تیغ من is a noun phrase 'the vengeful/vengeance-sword of mine' where کین (vengeance) functions as an epithet modifying تیغ (sword) and من marks possession ('my'). The whole sets up the content of the hope.
segments.s264-g30: سرت را is the definite object 'your head' marked by را. ز بن 'from the root/base' specifies the point of separation. بگسلاند is the transitive verb 'would/seemingly break off/sever' (here subordinate to امیدستم, so a desired/result clause). The final ز تن 'from the body' restates the separation source: 'sever your head from the body at the root.' Word order places object before the explanatory prepositional phrases for emphasis.
segments.s264-g31: که introduces a subordinate/result clause 'that/so that'. از خاک is 'from the earth' (source), and خیزد is the verb 'rises/comes forth' (3rd person singular present/future sense). ز خون تو 'from your blood' further specifies the material; گل 'a flower' is the thing that will rise. Word order (verb before subject or with subject final) is poetic but the sense is 'a flower will rise out of your blood from the earth.'
segments.s264-g32: یکی داستان is 'one tale/story' (numeral + noun). اندر is the preposition 'into/in'. آری here is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'brings' (3rd person singular) or 'may it bring' — used jussively/resultatively; this is not the common interjection 'indeed', but a verbal form (older Persian). به دل 'to the heart' marks the destination. The segment expresses the result: 'bringing one story into the heart.'
segments.s264-g241: به پیش نیا (prepositional phrase) = 'to the presence of Nia' (به + پیش used as 'to the presence of'); رفت is the past verb 'went' placed before the subject بیژن by poetic inversion (classical Persian often permits V–S order). چو دود begins a temporal clause 'when (there was) smoke' — چو = 'when', دود = 'smoke' with the verb omitted (typical in poetry).
segments.s264-g242: همی یاد کرد is a set phrase where همی gives iterative/continuous aspect ('kept / was') and یاد کرد = 'remembered' (یاد noun + کرد as light verb). آن کجا رفته بود is a subordinate clause in the pluperfect (رفته بود = 'had gone'); آن is emphatic/demonstrative before کجا 'where'.
segments.s264-g243: A list of three nouns (سلیح و سر و اسب) followed by the possessor هومان: this reads as 'the weapons, head, and horse of Hooman'. گرد here is an adverbial/lexical element (poetic) meaning roughly 'around/collected' or indicating the gathered items — it links these nouns to the action in the next segment.
segments.s264-g244: به پیش = 'to the presence of' (به + پیش); سپهدار گودرز is the title + name 'Commander Goudarz' (title precedes name). برد here is the past of بردن 'to take/bring' meaning '[they] brought / were brought' — in the provided senses list a matching verbal sense is not given, so the exact sense index is marked unknown.
segments.s264-g245: ز بیژن = 'because of / on account of Bijan' (ز = because of, a poetic preposition). چنان شاد شد پهلوان = 'the champion became so happy' — چنان = 'so', شاد شد = past 'became happy' (شد = become), and پهلوان (the subject) follows the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s264-g246: که introduces a result clause ('that / so that'); چنان ... که ... is 'so ... that ...'. گفتی here is used impersonally/poetically 'one might say' or 'it may be said' (the provided senses list does not show the verbal past 'said', so its index is left unknown). برافشاند is the verb 'to scatter/drive away' and خواهد is the future auxiliary 'will/ would' (here giving a modal/future sense), while روان is the object 'spirit/soul'. Word order is poetic/inverted (verb and auxiliary surround the object).
segments.s264-g247: گرفت آفرین is an idiomatic expression: گرفت (took/uttered) + آفرین (praise/exclamation) — 'he exclaimed praise' or 'he cried bravo'. پس = 'then'. به دادار = 'to the Creator' (به + دادار), and the final بر is the prepositional particle 'upon' that connects to the following phrase.
segments.s264-g248: بر آن اختر و بخت بیدار بر is a doubled, emphatic piling of prepositional praise: بر آن اختر = 'upon that star', و بخت بیدار = 'and (upon) awakened/alert fortune' (بخت = fate/fortune, بیدار = awake/alert). The final بر repeats the preposition for emphasis — overall an exclamatory formula praising celestial fate and destiny.
segments.s264-g105: پس از اسب هر دو فرود آمدند — 'پس از' here expresses a sequence/afterward, and 'از اسب' is the prepositional phrase 'from the horse(s)'. 'هر دو' (= 'both') is the subject, and the verb 'فرود آمدند' is a compound past tense meaning 'they alighted/dismounted' (فرود as noun/participle 'descent' + آمدند 'they came'). Word order is participial/PP then subject then finite verb.
segments.s264-g106: ز پیکار یکبار دم برزدند — 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' marking relation to the fight (پیکار). 'یکبار' qualifies the action as occurring once. 'دم' here is literal 'breath/moment' used idiomatically with برزدند; 'برزدند' is a past plural verb meaning 'they struck/struck out' or 'they made an attack/uttered (a cry)'. Together the clause says they made a single sudden action in the fight.
segments.s264-g107: گرفته به دست اسپشان ترجمان — 'گرفته' is a past participle 'having taken/seized' placed before the following phrase it governs. 'به دست اسپشان' ('in/into the hand of their steeds' or more idiomatically 'holding (the reins) in their hands'); 'اسپشان' is 'اسب' with the possessive suffix -شان ('their steeds'). 'ترجمان' follows as the item held/expressed (here used metaphorically). The sequence is participle + prepositional phrase + noun.
segments.s264-g108: دو جنگی به کردار شیر دمان — 'دو جنگی' is a nominal phrase 'the two fighters/warriors'. 'به کردار' is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner/character of' (به + noun for manner). 'شیر دمان' is a descriptive phrase 'lion-...' (poetic), so the whole phrase says the two warriors acted in the manner of lions. Adjectival phrase follows the head noun to describe their bearing.
segments.s264-g109: بدان ماندگی باز برخاستند — 'بدان' is an archaic/demonstrative-style element meaning 'to/at that' (hence 'at that remaining...'); 'ماندگی' is a noun 'remaining (time)/continuance' and together 'بدان ماندگی' = 'at that remaining pause/interval'. 'باز' is the adverb 'again', and 'برخاستند' is the past plural 'they rose/rose up'. Word order: temporal/locative phrase + adverb + verb.
segments.s264-g110: به کشتی گرفتن بیاراستند — 'به کشتی گرفتن' is a deverbal nominal phrase meaning 'to wrestle/grapple' (کشتی گرفتن = to take to wrestling/grappling); 'به' marks purpose/infinitive-like construction 'for/willing to'. 'بیاراستند' is a past-tense transitive verb 'they made ready/arrayed/armed themselves' (here: prepared themselves for grappling). The verb follows the infinitival phrase as usual in Persian.
segments.s264-g111: ز شبگیر تا سایه گسترد شید — 'ز' is the preposition 'from'. 'شبگیر' refers to the night/time around nightfall; 'تا' introduces the endpoint 'until'. 'سایه گسترد' is a verb phrase 'the shadow spread' (سایه = shadow, گسترد = spread). The final word 'شید' here is a poetic/nominal element (also used as an epithet) placed after the temporal clause; syntactically the clause sets the temporal span 'from dusk until the shadow spread.'
segments.s264-g112: دو خونی از این‌سان به بیم و امید — 'دو خونی' describes the two as 'blood-stained' (خونی = bloody). 'از این‌سان' = 'thus/like this'. 'به بیم و امید' is a circumstantial phrase 'in fear and hope' (به marking the relation). The whole segment summarizes their condition and emotional state during the span just given.
